sop.coo_evaluation:
# **Gaps — no SOP yet**

- **Member complaints & incident escalation** – no documented process for complaints (class quality, billing disputes, harassment, injury), severity triage, or resolution authority
- **Win-back/reactivation campaigns** – no SOP for identifying lapsed members, outreach sequences, or retention incentives
- **Coach performance management & feedback cycles** – no documented process for evaluating coach quality, member feedback loops, or improvement plans
- **Financial reconciliation & discrepancies** – no SOP for monthly P&L review, membership fee reconciliation, or refund/credit handling
- **Health & safety incident reporting** – no documented process for injury incidents, near-misses, accident reporting, or follow-up
- **Staff onboarding (non-coach roles)** – assuming admin/reception/management staff exist; unclear if documented
- **Member safeguarding & duty of care** – no clear process for reporting concerns (mental health, abuse, child safeguarding if applicable)
- **Pricing & promotion guardrails** – no SOP defining who can approve discounts, class packages, or promotional offers

---

# **Needs updating**

- **Email Newsletter & Blog Post SOPs** – these 2 of 58 aren't built; clarify frequency, approval workflows, brand voice, and content calendar ownership
- **Client onboarding** – likely missing: health screening process, PAR-Q or medical clearance SOP, orientation checklist for facility/app/payment methods
- **Class cancellation protocol** – no visible SOP for short-notice cancellations, member notification, or makeup class booking
- **Billing & membership pause/freeze** – likely incomplete: what triggers a freeze, member-initiated vs. staff-initiated pause, refund eligibility
- **Coach absence/cover scheduling** – unclear how last-minute callouts are managed and backfill assigned

kpi.advice:
# THE FORGE — FRACTIONAL CFO REVIEW & ACTIONS

## REVENUE — INCREASE

**Single biggest priority: arrest churn and stabilise membership.**
- **June churn 0% is excellent but masked by membership quality slide.** AR/M dropped to £120.48 (lowest on record; May was £165.56). You've grown headcount to 147 but members are paying less — likely new joiners on lower tiers or discounts to fill the bigger space. Audit tier mix: if >30% are on introductory/budget plans, raise entry tier pricing by 8–12% and cap discounted first-month offers to 2 months max.
- **Pricing drift is real.** Sep–Dec showed AR/M £145–£170; Jan–June ranged £120–£172. Lock in a standard pricing model (e.g. £24.99/month core, £39.99 premium) and review monthly.
- **Fill quiet class slots with 6am or 8pm sessions.** June revenue £17.7k on 147 members suggests underutilisation of the new studio capacity. Run utilisation audit: if <60% of peak-hour slots are full, add 1–2 off-peak classes and offer founding-member discounts (£15/month add-on for a 4th class per week) to upsell.

## EXPENSES — REDUCE

**Non-people costs are out of control.**
- **May £9,233 → June £9,390 non-people spend on lower revenue (£20.2k → £17.7k).** Drill into that line: utilities, rent, supplies, contractor costs. Compare June to May line-by-line. Suspect rent/lease just increased post-relocation — if so, negotiate a 3-year fixed rate or look for rent-sharing partner (PT studio, physio) to offset.
- **People costs £3,686 (June) on 147 members = £25/member. Target: £18–20/member.** That's 1–2 FTE too many for current revenue. Shift one instructor to variable/class-based pay (e.g. £35/class instead of salary) and consolidate admin/reception into one 20h/week role. Save ~£400/month.

## PROFITABILITY & TAX — IMPROVE

**Target 25% net profit margin by end of Q3; you're at 5.28% in June.**
- **Forecast Q3 (Jul–Sep).** Based on May/June run-rate and assuming no further attrition, model £18k

staff.advice:
• **Redistribute Grace's classes immediately:** Her 17 Build: Lower, 9 Build: Upper, 6 Elevate, and 3 Strongevity sessions need covering. JoJo already leads Build: Upper and Mobility; assign her the Strongevity (3 sessions) and rotate Build: Lower and Elevate to Eilis to build her confidence on non-Tuesday slots before progressing her.

• **Reduce JoJo's PT load:** At 44 PTs plus 113 other sessions, JoJo is over-extended. Transfer 15–20 of her PT clients to Eilis (who has only 11) to balance workload and develop Eilis's 1:1 coaching skills while keeping JoJo focused on technical class delivery.

• **Stop using coaches for Body Composition Scans:** These 40 scans (JoJo 20, Grace 9, Eilis 11) are admin, not coaching. Assign them to a reception or admin team member or rotate them as a quick, low-skill duty — frees ~6–8 real coaching hours per coach per month.

• **Address single-coach dependency on Circuits and Plate:** JoJo is the only Circuits coach (6 sessions); Eilis is the only Plate coach (4 sessions). Cross-train Eilis on Circuits (fits her development) and get JoJo or another coach qualified to cover Plate to avoid gaps if anyone is sick.

• **Aim for JoJo ~90–100 sessions, Eilis ~70–80 sessions post-rebalance:** This removes Grace entirely, spreads PT fairly, and gives Eilis sustainable growth in coaching breadth without overloading her on Tuesdays.

class_stats.suggestion:
**For quiet studio time gaps:**

1. Add a paid "Open Gym + Form Check" block on Monday/Thursday evenings (your quietest days) — 30 mins before or after existing classes — where members can book a quick PT form review at £15/session. Captures members already coming for other classes and fills dead studio time.

2. Run a weekly "Strength Basics Workshop" Saturday morning (your quietest day overall) — 45 mins on topics like "Deadlift Setup" or "Squat Mechanics" — invite Build class regulars and charge £8 or bundle free with memberships. Drives Saturday footfall and builds skills for your most popular classes.

**To lift the quieter classes:**

3. Move **Engine** to a Tuesday or Wednesday slot instead of its current quieter day — it's a conditioning class that will naturally attract Build attendees mid-week when gym traffic peaks. Also, run a 2-week "Engine Challenge" where attendees track metrics and winners get a free PT session; create a small group chat to build community and repeat bookings.

kpis.Action: INTERNAL: Create clear service menu (for coaches and for members) — what we offer, what it costs, what's included.
kpis.advice:
# Service Menu Execution for The Forge

**Get your coaches aligned FIRST (before members see anything):**
- Run a 30-min huddle with your coaching team — ask each coach: "What do YOU actually sell to members?" (You'll find contradictions. Fix those now or your menu dies on arrival.)
- Document what each service tier actually *includes* — not buzzwords, specifics: "1:1 session = 55 mins coaching + form video + Slack access for 7 days" vs. waffle like "personalized support"

**Build the member-facing menu as a **problem-solution matching tool:**
- Structure by outcome, not service type: "Get stronger" / "Build confidence" / "Compete" — then show which combo (group classes + 1:1 + programming) solves it + price
- Use your current best-case success stories as proof points next to each tier (not generic testimonials — *show* who this service is for)

**Price anchoring move:**
- Lead with your highest-margin offering first, price-wise (not lowest) — this makes mid-tier packages look like the smart choice
- Add ONE "entry" option at low price but with a clear cap (e.g., "drop-in only, no progression tracking") — weeds out tire-kickers, funnels serious people upward

**Lock it into ops immediately:**
- Give coaches a one-page "sales guide" showing how to pitch each tier based on what a member actually said in their intro (not a script — a framework)
- Test the menu with 3-5 members THIS WEEK as you're building it, not after

kpis.Action: EXTERNAL: Refine our funnel landing pages to make it very clear what we offer, look at common questions
kpis.advice:
# Landing Page Refinement for The Forge

**Pull your actual conversion data first** — identify which landing pages underperform and which traffic sources bounce highest. This tells you *where* confusion actually exists, not where you think it might. Check Google Analytics for pages where users land, scroll briefly, and leave.

**Audit competitor landing pages** (Equinox, local Belfast studios) and map their CTAs, value propositions, and FAQ sections. Steal their structure if it's working—don't reinvent. Grace, you're looking for what messaging *stops* objections, not what sounds pretty.

**Create a quick FAQ section based on real objections** — pull these from: member intake forms, email inquiries, cancellation reasons, and sales calls. Not "What are your hours?"—instead "Is this gym intimidating if I've never trained?" or "Do I need to book classes or walk in?" These are the friction points killing conversions.

**A/B test one headline change immediately** — currently, what's your main value prop? Change it to emphasize either (1) community/belonging, (2) results timeline, or (3) "no judgment" culture. Run it for 2 weeks. Measure sign-ups. This is faster than redesigning everything.

**Make the offer crystal clear above the fold** — "Join for £X/month" with *one* clear CTA button. If visitors can't answer "what does membership cost?" and "how do I start?" in 10 seconds, you've lost them. Simplify ruthlessly.

What's your current conversion rate on landing pages?

kpis.Action: Implement internal member feedback system (simple monthly form or post-class check-in)
kpis.advice:
# Quick Wins for The Forge's Feedback System

• **Start with post-class QR codes, not forms.** Place laminated QR codes by the exit linking to a 60-second Google Form (3 questions max: "What worked today?" "One thing to improve?" "Would you refer a friend?"). You'll get 3-5x higher response rates than email surveys—capture feedback when the endorphins are high and friction is zero.

• **Make it a retention play, not data collection.** Every 5th respondent gets a free class or coffee voucher. This turns feedback into a loyalty mechanic. Track which members respond repeatedly—they're your brand advocates. Reward them specifically.

• **Assign one person 30 mins/week to act on feedback publicly.** If members suggest "more 6am classes" or "fix the changing room lighting," post the action taken on your WhatsApp or Instagram story within 2 weeks with their first name. Members don't want to be heard—they want to see change. This closes the loop and doubles future participation.

• **Use the data to pre-qualify new service ideas.** Don't run a nutrition workshop unless your feedback mentions it 5+ times. This prevents wasted effort and shows members you're actually listening (not just surveying). Build your next revenue stream on demand, not assumption.

kpis.Action: Write SOP's for membership members, personal training members, fuel forward, well75, strongevity. Every service should have a clear SOP.
kpis.advice:
# SOPs for The Forge — Execution Plan

**1. Start with YOUR most problematic service first**
Don't write all five at once. Pick whichever service has the most member confusion, complaints, or staff inconsistency (my guess: Fuel Forward, given nutrition complexity). Write that SOP bulletproof first, then use it as your template. This prevents analysis paralysis and gives you a working model.

**2. Interview your frontline people, not just your memory**
Grab your PT staff and membership admins separately for 30 mins each. Ask: "What questions do members ask most?" and "Where do things go wrong?" Write the SOP to *actually solve these problems*, not what you think should happen. This makes it usable immediately, not shelf-ware.

**3. Structure each SOP the same way (one page max)**
- Member entry point (how they sign up/start)
- Weekly/monthly cadence (what happens when)
- Common questions answered upfront
- One escalation trigger ("When to call Grace")

This consistency means staff actually follow them. Add a "start date" so you know when it became standard.

**4. Make adoption instant, not aspirational**
Print one copy, laminate it, stick it where your staff actually work. Don't email a PDF. Get commitment: "This is the new way starting [date]—what blockers do you have?" Fix those blockers before launch day.

**5. Tie one SOP to a revenue metric**
For example: link "Personal Training Member SOP" to monthly PT retention rate. Review it monthly. This ensures they stay alive and get updated when reality shifts—not gathering dust.

kpis.Action: Establish a consistent social media content calendar with 3–4 posts/week planned in advance
kpis.advice:
# Social Media Calendar Execution for The Forge

• **Start with 4 weeks in a spreadsheet TODAY** — map posts to your actual class schedule + member wins. Columns: date | platform | content type | post copy stub | image/video source. This kills the "what do I post?" paralysis that kills consistency.

• **Batch-create content in 90-min sessions twice monthly** — film 8-10 short clips (30-60sec) of members mid-workout, transformation moments, or quick form tips in one studio session. You've got your visual library for a month. Phone camera is fine; authenticity beats production.

• **Assign ONE person (you or a motivated member) as "calendar keeper"** — someone checks the spreadsheet every Sunday and queues posts to Buffer/Later at off-peak times. This removes the daily friction that derails compliance.

• **Anchor posts to engagement hooks, not just posting** — don't just post; ask questions ("What's your non-negotiable workout day?"), use polls, tag members by name. Posts that drive 3-5 comments beat vanity likes and actually feed Instagram's algorithm.

• **Swap one generic motivational post weekly for a member story** — these convert better than anything you create alone. "Sarah went from zero to deadlifting 60kg in 8 weeks" with a before/after will pull more inquiries than 10 fitness quote graphics.

What platform matters most to your members right now — Instagram, TikTok, or Facebook?

kpis.Action: Schedule regular videography sessions every 8 weeks with Jordan
kpis.advice:
# Executing the 8-Week Videography Schedule with Jordan

**Lock it in NOW — not later**
- Text/email Jordan TODAY with 8 specific dates for the next 12 months (e.g., "Jan 15, Mar 12, May 7, July 9, Sept 10, Nov 12"). Non-negotiable calendar blocks for both of you. Vague "let's do it soon" kills momentum.

**Define the 30-minute output upfront**
- Brief Jordan on exactly what you need: 3-5 short clips (reels format) showcasing member transformations, class energy, or strength wins. Specify the hook in 3 seconds or people scroll. Get agreement on deliverables before he shows up, not during.

**Batch your content prep to coincide**
- Each session, have 2-3 members pre-selected who've had wins that cycle. Alert them Thursday before the shoot so they're mentally ready. Jordan captures faster when he's not hunting for stories on the day.

**Build a simple upload calendar**
- Schedule those clips to post 2x weekly (e.g., Tuesday/Friday) *during* the 8-week gaps. Don't hoard them. This keeps momentum visible between shoots and trains your audience to expect fresh content from The Forge.

**Track what actually converts**
- Note which video themes drive trial inquiries or membership questions. Share with Jordan before shoot #2 so he tilts toward what's actually moving the needle for you.

kpis.Action: Revisit systemize automations for 6 week trial enquiries
kpis.advice:
# Systemizing Trial Enquiry Automations for The Forge

• **Audit the current leak first** — Before rebuilding, track where trial enquiries drop off: initial signup → confirmation → first session → conversion. Grace likely loses 30-40% between stages. Fix the biggest leak before automating.

• **Build a micro-sequence, not a campaign** — 3-4 emails max over 6 weeks: (1) instant confirmation + gym tour booking link, (2) day before first session + what to bring, (3) mid-trial check-in at week 3, (4) conversion offer at week 5. Automation dies with bloat.

• **Lock in a text reminder 2 hours before first visit** — This single touchpoint converts 15-25% of no-shows. Use SMS (not email) tied to her booking confirmation. Zapier + Twilio integration takes 30 mins to set up.

• **Gate the "how to succeed" guide behind conversion** — Don't send tips/hacks during trial. Send a downloadable PDF only after they commit to membership. It removes the friction of *too much info* and signals investment required.

• **Assign one person (or herself) to manually check in at week 2** — Automation can't replace a genuine "How are you finding it?" message. This human touch at the critical midpoint is where trial → member decisions actually happen.

What's her current conversion rate from trial to paid membership?

kpis.Action: Finalise and document weekly action checklist for each role (JoJo, Eilis, Sarah) with KPIs they own
kpis.advice:
# Action Checklist Documentation — Execute Now

• **Create a shared Google Sheet (not a document)** with live columns: Role | Weekly Task | Success Metric | Owner Name | Status. Make it editable by all three so they update their own progress daily—removes you from policing and creates accountability friction naturally.

• **Pull last month's data first.** Before assigning KPIs, audit what JoJo, Eilis, and Sarah actually *did* (classes taught, sign-ups logged, retention calls made). Base their KPIs on their actual output capacity, not wishful thinking. This prevents them ghosting a goal that was never realistic.

• **One KPI per person maximum.** JoJo owns "weekly class fill rate %" | Eilis owns "new member conversion %" | Sarah owns "member retention calls completed." If you assign three metrics each, they'll abandon all of them. Specificity kills, vagueness kills faster.

• **Build in a 5-minute Friday check-in (not a meeting).** Each person Slack you their one number Friday EOD. You respond with one line: "On track" or "Let's talk Monday 9am." This keeps the checklist *live* instead of forgotten, and gives you early warning on drift.

• **Make their checklist their job description.** Send them the final version saying: "This is what success looks like in your role. Everything else is noise." Removes ambiguity on what they're actually employed to do.

**Do this today: Create the Sheet, pull your data, book the Friday Slack template.**

kpis.Action: Run team vision session — share the SSO, 90-day plan, and each person's lane clearly
kpis.advice:
# Run That Vision Session — Here's How Grace Executes It

• **Pre-session clarity (do this NOW):** Grace writes out her SSO (Single Significant Outcome) in one sentence. Then the 90-day plan in bullet points—max 5 initiatives. Each team member gets assigned ONE primary lane before the meeting starts. Walking in unprepared kills momentum; walking in with a one-pager means people leave aligned.

• **The session format (90 mins max):** 15 mins: Grace reads the SSO aloud and explains *why* (not just what). 30 mins: Walk through the 90-day plan, showing how each initiative connects to the SSO. 30 mins: Each person articulates their lane—what they own, what success looks like for them, what they need from others. 15 mins: Q&A on blockers only.

• **Make it stick:** After the session, Grace sends a one-page visual (can be a simple graphic or table) showing the SSO, the 90-day priorities, and each person's lane. No 10-page document—something people actually reference.

• **The real test:** In week 2, Grace asks each team member to tell *her* back their lane in one sentence. If they can't, the session didn't land. Fix it immediately in a quick 1-on-1.

• **Timing:** Do this *before* the quarter starts or before next Monday. The longer you wait, the less urgency sticks.

kpis.Action: Audit workload distribution — identify who is over-stretched and rebalance over first 30 days
kpis.advice:
# Workload Audit & Rebalance for The Forge — 30-Day Sprint

**Day 1-3: Map the actual workload (not job titles)**
- Have each team member time-log their work in 30-min blocks for 3 days. Don't ask them to estimate — you need real data. Focus on: class delivery, member onboarding, cleaning/maintenance, admin, social media, billing queries.
- Identify which tasks are *stealing* hours (e.g., someone handling member complaints on top of their main role).

**Day 4-7: Spot the breaking points**
- Look for one person doing 2+ roles badly instead of 1 role well (common in small gyms). Example: a class instructor also managing the front desk = both suffer.
- Flag tasks with zero backup — if one person leaves, the gym stops. This is your rebalance priority.

**Day 8-20: Create the swap**
- Reassign 1-2 specific tasks per over-stretched person to someone with capacity (even 5-hour shifts per week matter). Be explicit: "You stop handling cancellations; Sarah does that now."
- Cross-train one person on each critical single-person task *immediately* — don't wait for "ideal timing."

**Day 21-30: Embed & measure**
- Run it for 10 days, then debrief: Is member experience still solid? Are people actually doing their new tasks? Adjust *one more time* if needed.
- Lock in the new structure with a simple written reference (who owns what) posted where staff see it daily.

**The key**: If someone's been stretched for months, they won

# Cutting 10% Costs at The Forge — Specific Moves

• **Audit your utility spend THIS WEEK** — Women's gyms often overheat/over-cool. Get a smart meter reading, compare against industry benchmarks for your sqft, then negotiate with suppliers armed with competitor quotes. Typical savings: 15-20% on utilities alone without service cuts.

• **Renegotiate supplier contracts NOW** — Call your equipment maintenance, cleaning, and towel service providers. Tell them you're getting competing quotes (get 2-3 actual quotes first). Gyms typically see 8-12% reductions just by showing you're shopping around. Do this in the next 10 days before renewal dates pass.

• **Cut low-attendance class slots, not staff** — Analyze your booking data by time/day. Kill the 9am Tuesday spinning class if it's 3 people; consolidate into a high-attendance slot. This saves instructor hours (your biggest cost) while improving perceived class quality. You'll hit 10% without laying anyone off.

• **Reduce marketing spend on channels with <3% conversion** — Which Facebook/Instagram campaigns actually drive sign-ups vs. just vanity metrics? Defund the bottom 30% and redeploy that budget to your best-performing channel. Most gyms waste 25-30% of marketing budget on habits, not results.

• **Negotiate rates with your payment processor** — As a women's-only niche gym, you likely have negotiating power you haven't used. Call Stripe/Square, mention churn rate and member LTV, ask for 0.5-1% reduction. That's 5-8% of your transaction costs instantly

# Finance KPI Update — Execution Framework

• **Set a recurring 90-minute block (same day/time monthly).** Don't treat this as ad-hoc admin. Pick the 5th or last Friday of each month, lock it in your calendar, make it non-negotiable. This prevents the "I'll do it when I have time" death spiral.

• **Pull 3 numbers that actually drive decisions:** (1) Member acquisition cost vs. lifetime value ratio, (2) churn rate month-on-month, (3) revenue per member. Skip vanity metrics. These three tell you if the business is growing sustainably or just spinning its wheels.

• **Use a one-page Google Sheet template, not complex software.** Columns: target → actual → variance → action. If member revenue dropped 8%, you immediately write "investigate class attendance drop" in the action column. This forces you from numbers into decisions.

• **Automate the source data pull.** Get your POS/membership software to email you a summary report before your review block. You're spending 15 mins on data gathering, not 45.

• **Share ONE metric with your team weekly (2-min huddle).** Members see churn dropping? Tell them. Revenue up? Celebrate it. This keeps growth visible and makes KPIs a business discipline, not just your spreadsheet.

What's your biggest blocker right now — data accessibility or knowing which metrics matter most?

# Financial Forecasting Execution for The Forge

**Get your actual numbers first (today):**
- Pull last 12 months of bank statements + membership data. Don't estimate. You need real churn rate, seasonal dips, average revenue per member to forecast anything credible.

**Build a simple 3-scenario model (not complicated):**
- **Base case:** Flat growth, current churn continues, no new initiatives
- **Growth case:** +15% memberships from referral/retention push, +10% class pack sales
- **Risk case:** 20% member drop-off (recession hit, competitor opens nearby)
- Use spreadsheet templates—don't overthink this. Shows funders/investors you're thinking strategically.

**Identify your cash pinch points NOW:**
- Most gyms struggle July-August (summer holidays) and January (post-New Year drop-off). Map where The Forge historically dips. *That's* when you need cash reserves or alternative revenue (corporate wellness contracts, PT packages).

**Connect forecasting to one immediate decision:**
- Is this for securing funding? Deciding if you can hire? Planning expansion? Let that goal drive *which* numbers you prioritize forecasting. Don't build a 5-year model if you just need 6-month confidence for a hire decision.

**Who owns this:** You should do the initial model. Outsource spreadsheet polish to an accountant after, but never outsource understanding your own numbers.

kpis.Action: Map profitability of each service (classes vs PT vs add-ons) — build a simple per-service P&L
kpis.advice:
# Profitability Mapping for The Forge

**The Task Reality:** You need to know which offerings actually make money, not assume. Here's the execution:

• **Start with revenue buckets TODAY** — Pull last 3 months of actual sales data split into: group classes (by type if possible), 1:1 PT sessions, and add-ons (supplements, merch, etc.). Don't overthink categories. Excel is fine.

• **Assign DIRECT costs only** — For each bucket, calculate: instructor/trainer wages (pro-rata based on hours they work on that service), music/platform fees, equipment maintenance specific to that service. Skip overhead allocation for now—it clouds the picture.

• **The add-ons move fast** — Bet you'll find PT has the highest margin, but add-ons (protein shakes, branded gear) might be easier quick wins with near-zero labor cost. Identify which one first, then test scaling it.

• **One insight changes everything** — You'll likely discover one service subsidising another. Example: maybe classes look profitable until you factor in the instructor cost, but they're your customer acquisition engine. Once you *see* this, you can price/package strategically.

• **Thursday action:** Print the P&L, circle your highest and lowest margin services, then ask yourself: "Can I charge more for this, or cut its costs?" That's your next move.

What's your biggest revenue stream right now?

kpis.Action: Audit all active systemize automations — remove duplicates, confirm enquiry-to-trial flow works end to end.
kpis.advice:
# Audit & Fix Your Automation Stack – Concrete Actions

• **Start in Zapier/Make (whichever you use) – export a spreadsheet of EVERY active zap/automation TODAY.** Sort by trigger type (form submissions, email, tag changes). You'll spot duplicates immediately (e.g., two zaps sending the same welcome email to new enquiries). Kill redundant ones first – they're costing you monthly credits and creating confused customer journeys.

• **Map your actual enquiry-to-trial flow on paper RIGHT NOW:** Where do enquiry form submissions go? Do they hit your CRM? Email? A manual checklist? Do they get tagged? Does that tag trigger a welcome sequence? Trace it step-by-step. You'll find breaks (e.g., form captures lead but never gets tagged, so nurture sequence never fires). Fix broken links before adding anything new.

• **Test end-to-end with a fake enquiry submission TODAY.** Use a test email address you control. Does it land in your inbox AND your CRM? Does the auto-response go out within minutes? Does she get the trial booking link? If anything stalls, you've found your bottleneck. Document it.

• **Audit timing gaps:** How long between enquiry and first touchpoint? If it's >2 hours, you're losing conversions to competitor follow-up. Tighten the automation trigger to fire immediately. Check if your trial booking automation sends a confirmation + calendar invite + pre-class info pack. If missing, add those steps.

• **Set a 90-day review schedule NOW** – don't let this decay again. Monthly spot-checks on conversion rates by source (form vs. social vs. refer

kpis.Action: Pick 3 repetitive tasks and build an AI automation
kpis.advice:
# 3 Repetitive Tasks to Automate at The Forge + How to Execute

• **Task 1: New member onboarding emails** — Use Zapier + ChatGPT to auto-send personalized welcome sequences (day 1, day 3, day 7) triggered by payment confirmation. Template: fitness goals questionnaire → AI generates customized routine suggestions → links to your induction video. Takes 2 hours to set up, saves 5 hours/month.

• **Task 2: Class cancellation & rescheduling notifications** — Connect your booking system (Mindbody/Mariana Tek) to Twilio or native SMS. When a class cancels, members auto-get texts + alternative class links within minutes. You're not manually texting 20 people anymore. Set it up once, runs forever.

• **Task 3: Social media content reminders + caption generation** — Use Buffer + ChatGPT API. Schedule your weekly gym wins (member transformations, class highlights, motivational posts). Prompt ChatGPT with: "Write a 2-sentence Instagram caption for [class type] that speaks to busy Belfast women." Batch-generate 4 weeks of captions in 30 minutes instead of daily thinking.

**Start today:** Pick the one that wastes the most time *right now*. Don't build all three at once—you'll abandon it. Get one live this week, prove the ROI, then stack the next.

kpis.Action: Start Cyber security for forge website
kpis.advice:
# Cybersecurity Action Plan for The Forge Website

**1. Get a professional audit done THIS WEEK (outsource, don't DIY)**
- Contact a Belfast-based cybersecurity firm for a basic penetration test (~£500-£1.5k). This beats guessing what's broken.
- Non-negotiable areas: payment processing (if you take card details), member login system, and email capture forms.

**2. Implement the "quick wins" immediately**
- Force HTTPS on your domain (your hosting provider can do this in 10 minutes—ask them)
- Enable two-factor authentication on your admin login TODAY
- Change all default passwords on hosting/CMS accounts if you haven't already

**3. Get liability covered before launch**
- Add a basic privacy policy + cookie notice to your website (use a template generator like Termly or Iubenda—30 mins max)
- This isn't optional for a UK gym collecting member data; it's legal protection

**4. Set up ongoing monitoring without the headache**
- Install Wordfence (if WordPress) or equivalent for your platform—enables automated threat detection
- It logs suspicious login attempts and flags vulnerabilities; you get weekly reports without doing anything

**5. Assign ONE person (probably you initially) as the "security owner"**
- Pick 15 minutes monthly to review reports and update passwords
- Document who has access to what—this catches insider risks fast

**Why this matters for growth:** Data breaches kill gym memberships. One incident costs you trust + legal fees. Fixing this now = confidence to scale your marketing without risk.

What platform is your website built on?

marketing.advice:
**Insights**

Your cost per lead has deteriorated 350% from April to July (£6 → £27) whilst spend has collapsed 75% (£435 → £109). This isn't a budget cut working efficiently—it's a campaign in freefall. The close rate actually improved to 75% in July, so your sales team and offer remain strong; the problem is you've stopped reaching people. Spend dropped fastest between June and July, suggesting either budget starvation, ad account issues, or creative fatigue hitting hard.

Lead volume tells the real story: you went from 69 → 4 leads in three months. At July's £27 CPL, you'd need to spend £1,620 to hit April's 69 leads—but you're nowhere near that conversation. The profit per lead has actually held (roughly £100–140 per customer acquired), so scaling back isn't the answer; *not scaling* is killing you.

**Actions**

1. **Resurrect and test creative immediately.** You likely hit ad fatigue on existing creatives between April and June. Build 4–5 entirely new video/carousel ads: film real members mid-workout, testimonial-led pieces (25–35 age group especially), before/afters, and a direct-response angle ("6 weeks. Zero judgement. Transform here"). Launch these alongside any existing live ads at 50/50 spend split to isolate what's dead weight.

2. **Widen and refresh your audience stack.** Your July CPL suggests you're either targeting too narrow (lookalikes only) or your core audience is saturated. Test: (a) Broad-reach campaigns to women 25–45 in Belfast postcodes BT2–BT17 with purchase intent; (b) interest-stacking around fitness, female wellness, mental health; (c) new lookalike audiences built from July sales (smaller, fresher seed). Pause any audience that's been live >8 weeks.

3. **Commit £300–400 spend next month minimum.** At 75% close rate and £180–200 lifetime value per customer, your unit economics support aggressive scaling. Allocate: 60% to new creative tests, 30% to expanded
